content.problemStatement:
- Now that Git knows who you are, you’re ready to start using Git and actually track your projects.
- When we talk about how Git works, we come across three components — Working Directory, Staging Area, and Repository.


---

**Three Components**



- **Working Directory**: The working directory is the project folder on your system where your actual files and code exist.
Any new files you create or changes you make happen here first.

- **Staging Area**: The staging area is a temporary storage inside Git where you place selected changes that you want to include in the next commit.
- **Repository**: The local repository is the hidden storage that contains the complete history of your project and all committed snapshots.


**Both staging area and local repository data are stored in the .git folder.**



--- 

**Example Scenario**

- You have three files in your VS Code / working directory on your local machine:
```dfgsf
You edit three files:

index.html  ✅ final changes
style.css   ⏳ still working on it
app.js      ✅ ready to save
```

- You can stage only the ready files(moved to staging area):
```sfgs
index.html  ✅ final changes
app.js      ✅ ready to save
```

- After you are sure, you can commit everything present in your staging area to the local repository.
